ayoreo: Chi toi mu chise jnani bajade uñai,*queyayode*uñai. I tu Abram nanique. Chi chise iji pijogadi uté uje Dupade anguretasórone jogadi u.
english: But Pajei and the others just didn’t understand*Dupade*’s*Word*, and that’s why they acted like that.

ayoreo: Yacaranguipis ome ua uje yudúco baruode iji dirode jnese. Yacaranguipis ome ua uje bajopié tu uje déji yocajei. Ua u uje bapesute yocáyipiedie aja quigode.
english: *Bajopié tu uje déji yocajei*

ayoreo:
Jecute déji yoque gajneri,
Espíritu Sánto, uje chisi ome yoque ome yoquirajatiguei. Yirajágo cuchade jnese
ome yocatoi, yoquirajatiguei, uje Espíritu Sánto ẽracáidode cucha. Mu uñane
déji cojñone uje que ore chijna go. Dupade chisi ome ore bisideque ore
irajatiguei. Enga que cuchárique tṍrapísiji go. Ore chijna ome Satanas
atuatigade uje Dupade que pota go. Satanas que chijna Dupade ẽracáidode cucha,
mu jé basa ga yocuñane ga yijnángo Dupade ẽracáidode cucha.
english: But we thank Dupade that we are using Dupade’s gifts so that God won’t lose what he’s given to us humans that he created. Perhaps he will bless us now and cause his power, the Holy Spirit, to come upon us. He told us that his power would come, and he said: “The Holy Spirit is the one that will help you and give you knowledge and understanding.”

ayoreo:
Enga
yicatecã́ri Dupade ome uaque to, yurasade uaque á. Yutai Dupade ujetiga uaque
uñeque changureta to enga angoninguesã́tigo déji uaque.
english: And for you, my countrymen, I hope that you believe also, and have obedient hearts and open ears.

ayoreo: Ore chicáji. Ga ore tujúnapo danochade rĩ, jé aja ore ayode, jé aja potayedie, jé aja ore cóbidie, ore ibotadie. Uje iyataque cho potírique ga qué turuapo dabai te rĩ. Ore chapúsaja.
english: An Ayoré woman might take the fruit herself to her husband and he eats what she offers to him.

ayoreo:
Enga adi jec
u ga gosi chise Dupade iji gatajnoquei ga chimo dojórigo iji*jnaropié*te
ga, gajño pioi te ga, a uñeque chojningárása: “Dupade á, jé go que ango ore
iji Tobité te ga, jé ca ore chicáji gajño pioi uté to.”
english: Dupade – God

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
Chi
chojninga: “Boyo gajnerĩ, acayoi erámi.” Ga chi ore jno, ga ore chacate
cojñone iji erámi. Cho ore quique ome cucha dacadode. Cho ore quique ujetiga ca
ore tangári cojñone ore guetetigade ome raque.
english: I’ll never forget*Dupade’s**Words*that he taught to his disciples and said to them: “Go and bring all the people, all kinds of tribal people and teach them.” And his disciples obeyed him. They took off and went out into the world. He also said something else to his disciples – and they listened to him. They really listened to their teacher’s lessons because they wanted to hear what he had to say.

ayoreo:
Ga jnacari chi chojniga: “Berané
casé chequé ome yu, aniri, tagu barengué pore adie diríca que, enga chisi ome
yu to que, ga agu to que, mu yiba tu anira, berané, bapesudé ica, chequé.”
english: Then the young man said: “That woman that you gave me, well, she ate that fruit first and then she gave some to me, and I ate it too, but it was because of that woman you made for me.”

ayoreo: Ojningai u uyoque ujetiga ca yoco yocáyugu ome cuchade unojo. Uje yoquijmainie cuse enga uyoque urásu ujetiga yipéco yoque enga yisago yoquejórigo ome yoquipotigátigo cucha. Jé uacamóño to uje chugúperenie que jmanéraque cuse. Jé uacamóño uje cucha pojnánguenie que jmanéraque cuse mu e ueradei ore. Chamáñaje Dupade u ore ipesungóri. Ore jmainie u Dupade, enga Dupade ipesute ore ueratigade. Dupade chosóre yoque to ga cho dajnúji yoque uje pésu ayoréode uyoque iji taningai nanique.*(Mateo 6:31-33)*
english: We are the ones he is referring to. He wanted us to stop worrying about our physical needs.*Dupade*has given us hands so that we could have the means of grooming ourselves and working to provide ourselves with the things we need. You see how the birds and the flowers don’t have a means of providing for themselves, because they were made without hands. But the birds have food, and the flowers have beauty. That’s because*Dupade*is their creator – he is their hands and he is the one who makes them beautiful. God provides for us also for we were made in his image in the very beginning.*(Matthew 6:31-34)*

ayoreo: Chi iñoñai ore sué iñoñai gai gu.
english: It was*Jaboidé*who told us that*Poritai*was the one who made warriors.

ayoreo: “Uacamóño to,” chojninga, “uje ayoré dacasutedie pijositodie u udiji ore.
english: The*Picanerane Clan*hit him, and like the*Posorajane Clan*, they didn’t suck their breath in and out.

ayoreo: *Umaridaté*chojninga: “¡*Jocha*casicaite de a tí!”
english: I said to myself, “I’ll soon reach the other women.” And I did. I found where they had gotten honey, and I found some also and carried it with me. I saw them at a distance. I saw them and right away I saw that they had seen me too. When I arrived I told them I had slept where someone had made a grave, and at that time I had no idea how close they were.

ayoreo: *Ecarai – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Marina churúsãre Ecarai acajnaquéode udojo nanique*
english: Dupade – God *Ecarai – Tobité,Bolivia – 1970’s**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: *Rincóngosi – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Rincóngotique uté uruode.*
english: When he arrives, it will be with fire that will ignite and burn. And it will be aflame all around us.*Dupade*’s*Word**s*are very strong.

ayoreo:
Ore chisi ome ore ugúchade ome ore: enuéone, duchúbiedie,
paode.
english: The object of the game, besides winning prizes, is to keep in fit condition. The losers give the winners from their possessions, or they hunt honey for the winners. Among the things given would be:  bags, gourds, rope, honey gourds, tapir hide shoes.

ayoreo: E gusu yacajnaquéode udojo.*(Jeremías 29:11, 12)* *Ecarai – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Marina de Morarie churúsare Ecarai uruode udojo*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**.*

ayoreo: Uje gosi chirajapise enga jorane ore chojninga: “¡Que pinguíraja jnumi gotique!”
english: If a person is especially good at wrestling, this is said of him:  “He’s invincible.”

ayoreo: Yitodódi u Dupade ujuyaquei cuchábi mu que toque go jetiga jé yise.  Ga que yise yiquigade ga que yáta yu aja puágueraque go gajneri. Satanas ipesutique puáguerac. Jiei iji erámi uje ayoredie ore chotigase. Yajéngome ore uruode.
english: *Señora Marina*talked to*Dijaide*about this and told him: “Haven’t you heard that we who believe and follow*Dupade*will be persecuted? And that is what happened to*Dupade*’s Son Jesus also. And he said: “I have done nothing wrong and yet they will kill me and nail me to a cross,” and he said, “how much more will they persecute you who are my disciples.”

ayoreo: *Betidaté – Tobité, Bolivia –**08/23/75.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre uruode nanique enga cháta Dupade uruode aja to.*
english: *Betidaté’s – Tobité, Bolivia – 08/23/75**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**Scripture portion added.*

ayoreo: Yibatigai jogadi Engábi u guidábi nanique. Iapade i tu*Jomainé*. Ité i tu*Podé*. Direquedéjnagose yacoté dai ga i tu*Igoi*. Daté i tu*Jumuné*.
english: The place where I was born was called*Engábi;*that was the name of the little village. My father’s name was*Jomaine.*My mother’s name was*Podé*. My wife’s father was a*Direquedéjnagosi*, and his name was*Igoi*. Her mother’s name was*Jumuné.*

ayoreo:
San Pedro uruode u udojo ome
yoque. Cho yoquiquique jeta ca yachógo Jesus diringai yui.
english: I don’t know if what I am teaching you is ugly to you. But it’s what Jesus said to us about becoming believers. He said, “Be strong. Even though your faith is small, it’s still good. It will be a very good thing in the future for the person who has put his faith in me and has loved his fellow-ayoréos.”

ayoreo: Yocacaranguipise gajiné uje bisidi déji ome yocate gajnaréngone enga bisidi u Jesus iyoi. Yicoi ga yutõcoi Jesus jnese ujetiga chĩro yoquisijnaringuéone, enga ca yipójo yocate gajnarétique uñéngome uté: jé ca yãrájo yoque jne.
english: Oh, how grateful we should be that there is a remedy for sin. I think it was Peter who spoke to Jesus and said, “Lord, what should I do about the people who sin against me? Shall I ignore sin, or forgive them?”

ayoreo: Ga yico. Yicagóji dojo. Ga anire, yicoji ta, degúrase ta. Mu cho ta degúrase ji de.
english: We took off. We went over ‘there’. And then, we went another direction toward the camp nearby.

ayoreo: Yoque Dupade, yicatecã́ri ua gajnerĩ uje yacaranguipise ome ua. Mu, yoque Dupade, yicatecã́ri ua gu jetiga ango yocurasade udojo uñéngo ujetiga ore changureta ua enga orei óriji gatajnoquei to jne. Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipise ome ua gajnerĩ uje baruode déji erámi údode jnese iji de deréjnai jnese, ujetiga banguretasórone ore chacate darásarigo ayoré iji erámi. Yoque Dupade, ãro pajei dotanigane, pajei jũruachuringane ore iji erámone jnese ome bajopié. Yoque Dupade yicatecã́ri ua gajnerĩ ome yocurasade ore uje ore catecã́ri ua, mu que yimo jeta ore changureta bajnacari, Yoque Dupade. Que yimo jeta ore chiraja pujnuéngaisṍri uhua, Yoque Dupade. Mu átaja udore gajnerĩ ujetiga ore changureta ua enga ca ore cho sebégome dáringárique Dupade anguretasórone gajnerĩ, Dupade.
english: That is all I have to teach us. I want you to know, though, that those of my village are always remembering you in prayer, asking*Dupade*who loves us everywhere on earth that we might all go together to heaven. That is all my teaching.

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “¿Bayode guejnato isocárigo cho rique? Chi chojninga: “Yayode guejnato a di jne,  ga bajé usereréjna aja iquei, úyujna cuchábe bajé aja iquei. Mu chi bajaque jnacode, chugúpẽre yote dequejnangórone aja iquei jne. A di jne.”
english: He ran till he reached the large village of the **Chamacoco tribe*who are friends of the Ayoré. It’s said he fell down when he got to their village square. And it’s said they all got down on the ground (where he’d fallen).

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
“Que yiji yurupatáringa,” chojninga. “Que yipésu
cucha gajnarétique yúi ga jé iji yúringai disi aja yúringai jnacare.”
english: He said, “I’ve never killed anyone. I’ve never lied. I’ve never done anything wrong since I was a little boy,’ he said.

ayoreo: Uñane ore chojninga: “Yujé ga yijna acoté aja yu.” Ore timíninga aja ajesúode, abai, jeta ore chujé. Mu chiraja ore, acoté, ga chojningame dabai: “¡Boyo yico!”
english: But Lizard’s relatives defended him and so they weren’t able to kill him. They said to*Corabé’s*husband: “We think it’s best if we leave here and take her with us!”

ayoreo: Jé uacamóño ayorérique a ca chujé dojórique ayoré, que pésu cucha gajnarétique, mu que changureta Dupade. Dupade que jno údi go. Jecute uqué cojñoque ugucharãi chirase dáringai ugucharãque. Que chiraja Dupade uruode yúrique go. Mu chise gójni diri tudé enga ujetiga chicai gajño pioi te ga, cho jóroque dáyugu re, jnusina jóroque dáringai cojñoque ugucharãc. Jnusina jóroque dáringai ayoré subudé iji jnumi uje que chimo chaguhei ome re nanique, mu que toc jeta qué chisapo ayoré ijõre iji, aníri, iji pitoringai ejoi.
english: But the young man loved being well off.  He loved being rich, more than he loved*Dupade*.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Beuidé, Noainé ore.
english: Their chiefs were:*Beuidé,*and*Noainé*.

ayoreo:
Yocacaranguipis ome ujnojo uje
yirajatigai garósi mu que Dupade chachia yoquirajatigai gotique. Jé yisíngome
yocujode ayoré yoquirajatígabi enga Dupade irasique re enga chojninga:
“Barajatigai garósi marínga, jé angome bojode ayoréo uje uacanguretáyo.”
english: Even though there are few of us, we know that he is pleased with us. His word says: “Even though a person might be alone in bed, or alone on a trip, I will help that person. I will help him if he listens to me.”

ayoreo: Jé uacamóño uje que yiraja cojñotique urúrique to. E ueradé yocuñane. Mu etoguipise Dupade. Etoque Dupade. Mu Dupade anguresóningo, anguretórigui – chequedíe, gapudíe ore – agúsoi Dupade to. Adotáyo uágaja. Dupade anguresórone etoc ore ome cuchade. Etoc ore gu uje que ore pota cucha quedéjnarique gotique.
english: You know that I do not understand Spanish. Some of us are royal. But*Dupade*is more royal and very strong. But*Dupade*’s believers, men, and women, you are in*Dupade*.  Be firm and unmovable.*Dupade*’s believers have his strength for all things. They are strong because they want only*Dupade*and desire nothing else.

ayoreo: *Borade – Asõrenie Ore*
english: *Borade – Asõrenie Ore*

ayoreo: Jé ca ajéchome Dupade adode to jeta acacho yoque to. Jnacarode uhuac, jé gapudíe uhuáque to. A jé ca ajéjome Dupade adode. Yacaco ore iji dirode jnese to.*(1 Timoteo 4:12)*
english: I’m so thankful to Dupade that he remembers us all the time, even today. He’s the same all the time. Let’s don’t consider each other sinners, we men and women who believe in Dupade. Let’s don’t find fault with each other, we who believe in Dupade.

ayoreo: Ore asuté u: Uniné.
english: Their chief was:*Uniné.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie**churúsãre Enueidaté atatagode udojo.*
english: *Enuedaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
Mu Adã acoté Eba chajire poria
tuaté údi, uburúdi iji dirode uñeque. Enga anire. Eba qué chajire poria tuaté uburúdi
ga chajire gãrengué casé pore uburúdi ga chi chijire adie. Eti chi Eba chi
chudute darade enga chi chijire darade mu uga. Enga chi ugai chi narare. Enga
chi chojnirame Eba, ayoré bajé: “¿Gotique uje que agu poria tuaté adie á?”
english: But then, one day Adam’s wife Eve noticed how shady it was beneath that tree. And then this is what happened: When Eve was in the shade of the tree and enjoying it, she noticed its fruit. And then, she heard someone speak and she looked toward the voice and it was coming from a snake. It was speaking to her. And it said to Eve, the very first woman: “Why don’t you eat some of that tree’s fruit?”

ayoreo:
Enga udojo u uje cáma yicoi to*Dupade*ojningane. Uje chugúperámia
chojninga: “Acho ujetiga aríño, ujetiga e ore chaquesu uaque.” Enga jec ũré
Jesus chojninga to: “Mu acho arurégode enga asáchoja yiríringárique jne ujetiga
amesẽcho yatígo,” chojninga.
english: This is what Jesus told his disciples to do: ‘Drink this juice until I come again,’ he said. He said: ‘Eat this bread until I return, and then I’ll know that you haven’t forgotten me,’ he said. But we haven’t been doing this thing that*Dupade*has told us to do.

ayoreo: Jnani bajade ore chujé*Togábi*nanique uje i uñai u*Goródedé*. Ga chi ore chujé. Ga chi daijna ujopierãque  uté  nanique.  Ga chi*Ucatédé*(ore asuté), jõrai*Baráyudé*ga ore u uje ore chujé*Togábi.*
english: *Togábi*was killed by men who lived many years ago*.*(He was also called*Gorodédé.) Togábi*was a medicine man, but one that could curse people. They say it was*Ucatédé*(their chief) and his friend*Baráyudé*that killed*Togábi*.

ayoreo:
Ore jno ga
ugucharãque ore. Que ore chojningame dacotedie, chi ca ore chojningame: “A qué
yiriapo jne dirome rĩ.” Ore áyipie gaidi u ujetiga ore chise dacuegode
ayoréode ore uyoque gu. Enga
ore chise ore jne jeta ore chingome ore ome Jesute to. Uje Ayoréode ore,
yirajágo iji dirode udojo uje eduguéjnane chubu erámone gu, anire, uje ore
chosõre yoque ome yoquisijnaringuéone iji udé yocuniri.
english: It was because of their wanting to be kind to us Ayoré that the missionaries kept going out to look for us. And they found signs of the Ayoré. And so they kept searching without giving much thought to the fact that the Ayoré might kill them.  They were trusting in*Dupade*and felt that he could save them – even in Ayoré territory.

ayoreo: Mu*Ijnamene*chojninga: “Cachi ayipieraque uje yujo gapua déji ejoi mu jé chujñieta yu gu. Yiya. Yiabi yujo Dosapeque ome yabai,*Oregajnúi*.”
english: Some girls came out to meet me and told me things. They said: “*Ijnamene*, you’ve arrived, but*Jup*i is following you, and wants you to go with him into the camp.”

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: There was a dispute involving*Arangué*and during the battle the*Tiegosode*killed some of the*Totobiegosode*and that’s when they separated from each other. The chief of the*Tiegosode*was*Jochadáquide*. Because*Arangué*and others of her*group*were eating bean seeds that had been saved for planting,*Jochadáquide*sent people to kill them.

ayoreo:
Enga
Saulo jorane jnanio chi ca ore chimo Jesuse to go. Gusu uje ore
chudute darárigo enga tṍraji gaté. Ga chi Jesuse chajuque udore ore
agutedie. Chi gusu Saulo uje chudute darade ga chojninga, chi Saulo chojninga:
“¿Jé ua u gosi a?”
english: That is how it is if a person sees Jesus but doesn’t believe he is*Dupade*,*Dupade*takes away his sight, his spiritual sight.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Guié iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Guié – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: *Enueidaté bijna nanique enga chise yodi iji poridie:*
english: *Jocha – Enuedaté’s name before she had children *Enuedaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*
*Amomégosode***–**“People of*amomé*.”

ayoreo: Chiese yãcorenie chiraja ayoréode uyoque ga u oidi jnani bajaque ayoré ga chi chojninga: “Boyo iji ayoréode uyoque uje jocãratade udore gu. Ga boyo.”
english: Later on he killed ‘big-bellied pigs.’ And when he was a young man he would kill whole troops of wild pigs. And that’s when he became known as the great hunter of wild pigs. And it’s also when the wild pigs got fed up with him because of how many pigs he was killing.

ayoreo: Chi Cochocoigosode ore chicáji Jnupedogosode nanique iji diri uté nanique uje chuque misionérone iji. Jiei bajade uje e ore jno, mu uñane qué ore sase. Ijmai déji bajade ore ujai nanique enga jno to. Mu Caratábide chicáji dejnai bajaque ga déji uté uje chudute cuchárique. A misionérone u enga ore catecã́ri ñane uje ore chicáji uté yui. Todo ga chayo ga chicáji degúi ujetiga chingo darásade iji uté. Chojninga: “¡Cojñotígo e di é, ayóyome ore!” Enga urasade ore chayo jnese.
english: The men from the*Cochocóigosode*grouop were going off to the*Jnupedógosode*the day the men were killed. Many of them had already left and others were getting ready to follow.*Ijmai*was in the group who had already left.

ayoreo:
Dupade chi di jne, enga chicha
yógaja dajmana ajã́ro. Jé uacamóño to uje yoquijmana ajãro ajãpe. Ajãpe ga a
yicha yujórique ayoré disírique aja ga chapédugu. Dupade chi di jne, enga
chicháji ua iji dajmana ajã́ro jne jé aja cojñone, jé aja cuchisode ore
uyoque. Jé cho yoque júruru. Jé uacamóño uje ajãpe yoquijmanai ga ayoré chojmarai chapédugu.
Dupade cho dajuñame yoque uje chi chicha yógaja dajmana ajã́ro jne. Chamáta
cuchisode ore uyoque. E uacamóño uje e jiei cuchisode, cojñone ore uyoque.
Itoróngaja. Chi itoróngaja jmana ajã́ro. A di jne enga chujé ibiotígo tese rĩ. Tagu cuchade enga chejna cuchade
udore jnese. E tagu yoque júruru. Etóguipise Dupade Uruode.*(Hebreos 12:29)*
english: The Lord is coming again, and when he does return, he’ll take us in his hands. Our hands are so small in comparison to his. My hand is so small that were I to try to fit even a small child into it he would be too big for it. When*Dupade*comes, he will easily fit you into his hands along with all the*cojñone*. Along with us, he’ll gather the animals also. And you know there’s many animals, cojñone and us. And yet with all of us, there will be room to spare. We won’t be crowded at all in his hand.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre piradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger*

ayoreo:
“Jec ũré enga uacarajáyo Yapade,” chiese
Jesus chojninga. Chujé yógaja jnumone ome darúo.
english: And, in my dream that night, I prayed, and I said: “*Dupade*! I’m talking to you right now. I’m very thankful that your Son Jesus appeared to me and talked to me about you, and that is why I am talking to you about myself, because I’m in lots of pain and I ask you to help me.” This is what I seemed to say to*Dupade*in my dream.  “Do something about my sickness, please,” I said in my dream, praying to*Dupade*. “I’m so thankful that your Son Jesus appeared to me,” I said to him.

ayoreo: Uje quene yicagóji Tobité nanique iji taningai, enga ité, yinanie, uyoque yujégo yogaja buéyode gáyode. Uje yirõco Tobité yui, mu yai yusṍrone ore chachai yoque. Uje ore chudute uje chi ore yui acoté chicáji Tobité enga ore pota jeta ore chachai uaté. Uje yĩríngo enga ore chojningame ité: “Yoco yocáyugu uje yipéco bajnusiétigade nanique. Ujetiga yirajágorase uje ueradei babai ore áyipiedie nanique enga, a ca yujégorase ore.”
english: The first time we went into Tobité‚ my mother, my sisters, and I had to ride oxen.  As we were arriving, we were met by my father’s killers. When they heard that the wife of one of their victims was coming into camp, they wanted to go out to meet her. When they met her, they said to my mother: “We are very sorry for the sorrow we have caused you. If we had known then what we know now, we would never have done it.”

ayoreo: *Jnaniné*uyu yojninga*:*“Ẽ́*Chequé Dago*, ãrame yu. Yijina yurasade ore to. Deroco yui jeta tṍra yugúchade, yacoté. Babe yu gu,” yojningame*Chequé Dago*. “Be bajogadi gotique ga yimóji yijogadi. Jnani yu. Yabode déji degúi.”
english: *Cheque Dago*came to our village, and*Cheque Dago*took shelter where I was in the shade. (But*Jnaniné*wouldn’t let her in.)

ayoreo:
Enga
chojninga, “Yapá, cáma yijnángui yoc uñeque. Mu Satanás u uje jno yocuñai
casica údi. Mu baruode ojningane jé u,” chojninga. “Uje chingo yoc ujetiga
ojningane cucha tocade,” chojninga.
english: I think you know who Jesus was referring to when he said that Satan would take one of the disciples and cause him to die. That disciple would throw himself down from a high mountain, and his intestines would burst out of him.*(Acts 1:18)*

ayoreo: Udojo u uje jec ũrépise Pablo ome dacajnaquéode uje chojningame suté jnani bajai Tito. Chojninga: “Tito á! Yijíñome ua, aníri, iji ore idai uje i tu Creta, yotequedéjnai ajã́ro,” chojninga. “Ujetiga acate ore iji,” chojninga. “Uje cáma yingo*Dupade**Uruode*uñane aja ore to ica. Angome ore enga ore doi to,” chojninga. “Ore changureta to*Dupade*ujetiga jec ũré enga ango ore,” chojninga.
english: But if a person confesses his sin to*Dupade*, then*Dupade*does something very special for that one. He forgives his sin, and that person is completely well and clean again.

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*Tie Quedéjnai*, ga jiei ore.
english: *Direquedéjnagosipísode (Unirequedéjnagosode) –*“Original people of the strange day (land).”

ayoreo: Uje pésu ore ájningane iji ore ajei uje chi ore chujé jnani bajade ore iñoñai, ome*Jaboidé*. Jé u uje dacasutedie cucherane chi ore ipesungóri tu ore iñoñai (*Poritai*). Chi dacasutedie ore ipesungóri udé, ome*Jaboidé*.
english: There are other things we’ve been told that pertain to Ayoré warriors.

ayoreo: Eba chi doi aníri dicoré uruode ga chi chisa poria áraque enga jec u uje chi tagu. Enga Eba chi chisiapome dabai to rĩ. Enga Adã chi tagu to. Enga jec u uje chi ore jnusina uje e ore chimánaja dajei uje chi Dupade chetaque poria to ome ore ujetiga ore tagu. Chi ore jnusina uje Dupade chi anire, gãrengué diríca, enga chi jec u uje que ore chiraja daru quitígo ome Dupade gotique. Uje ore tagu gãrengué casé pore adie.
english: So Eve did exactly what he told her to do and she picked fruit from the tree and she took a bite from it. And then she gave some to her husband also. And Adam took a bite of it, too. And then it was that they remembered why God had not wanted them to eat from that tree. They remembered that God had forbidden it to them, and they didn’t know what they were going to say to God about what they had done. For now they had eaten what he had forbidden them to eat – the fruit from the tree.

ayoreo: Chi daijnane ore chujé dajnu ditáji ga pujopie tṍraji ore ome ore enga jé daijna ore.
english: The medicine men bite to pull venom out of the one who has been bitten by a rattlesnake. The medicine men are powerful. To heal people who have been bitten they pull the venom out of them. According to the medicine men who lived long ago, power comes to the medicine men from the twisted wild pineapple plant’s fibers. They open themselves up and power presents itself to them from the twisted wild pineapple plant. By using the twisted wild pineapple plant, those who are already medicine men are able to bestow power to heal on other men.

ayoreo: Yigaidi casé yujórique ayoré. Gajnáreque udé ga que Dupade acho gotique. U gapudíe uhuac, jnacarode ore. Angáchoi yocacadingórone – Guejnáminé ore.
english: We’re so thankful to Dupade that Ipeyai and the others are with us, and they are telling us about Dupade. You who are Dupade’s believers, even if you have been doing ugly things in different ways, don’t be ashamed to confess these things. It’s wrong when we are hesitant to speak about them. Because if a person doesn’t take care of his sins, when Dupade returns, Dupade will be ashamed of that person.

ayoreo: Jecute que toc jeta jé yicadigui. Yáyipie gaidode u unojo uje que yiramurase Dupade iji yajei. Yatecã́ri iji dejade jnese. Que yiramurase rĩ uje*yugúsiji*cojñone ujade, iji yujode ayoré ujade rĩ. Uje yajire cojñone iji erámi, yosõre ore to, mu ome Dupade Uruode mu que toc ujetiga yécãre ore atique.  Gusu Dupade Abi ujetiga chécãre ore áyipie quitique. Ore chisi re ome Jesucristo to ga churu ore ate gajnaréngone. Ga jec u ujetiga chojmára ore iji jnumi.
english: I’m so grateful for all the ways that*Dupade*has helped me here in this world. He has never forgotten me. And I have not forgotten his Word that says: “I will go with you till the end of the world. Your fellow men might persecute you, the people of the world do you wrong, but I will be with you always.”

ayoreo: *Icadiguédé*, ore iroguebeque, ore chajmára iradedie.
english: The songs  of **Icadiguédé*are some that we really look forward to hearing! People are always asking him to sing.

ayoreo:
Enga Moises
tangáripísi*Dupade*enga jĩrique
chejñu darídade.*Dupade*chojningame
nanique: “Ca ajose yi. Ejñu bachiédode iji ua uje puyaque yiyaridi unojo gu.”
Uje Moises chejñu darídade enga jé u uje*Dupade*chajnéra daruode údi ome Moises, ga chojningame: “Uacayode Abraham, Isaac,
Jacob ore angureni casica Dupa jé uyu uje badute yuruode iji de.”
english: *1 Corinthians 14:40)**Dupade*spoke to Moses when he appeared to him in a burning bush, and he said: ‘Before you approach the bush that has fire coming from it, take off your shoes, and then come toward me.’ That’s what*Dupade*said.

ayoreo:
Mu udé
deréjnai ga Pablo que cãra dabosode ome uaque gotique. Jé basa ga jnani bajai
chi ugucharãque, mu que chajire Dupade anguretasṍri yúrique go.
english: These days Paul Wyma has been sharing what food he has with you, not holding anything back. But the rich man I am talking about who lived long ago, had a lot of money, but he didn’t pay any attention to the poor man who believed in*Dupade*.

ayoreo: Yiquejnángome jocaratade ga*Ajópedái*chise yóguiji ta. Mu jé gaidirase ore iji degúi. Mu*Oguéa*,*Usigaidatéa*ore chaquĩ́ra. Ore chojninga: “Ácari te enga bo.”
english: Two returned to the camp. And we went far away. We kept running from the warriors and ran far away.

ayoreo:
Enga
yacajnaquéode adi jé u to ome uaque uje*Dupade*chetaque ichorade iji daguíjnai ajei.*Dupade*imeserãi tu ujetiga yoco paráguiji uruode. Chamáñaje obiréjna ome*Dupade*gu uje yijnórango uté, enga que
yoco cucha bisidéguiji uruode, uje yoco paraque gu iji iguíjnai.
english: But I think I have taught you many things from*Dupade*’s*Words*, except the things that are hard and that I don’t fully understand yet. I haven’t taught you about them. But the day will come that I will teach you these things; that is, if*Dupade*gives me understanding, and if Jesus has not yet returned, and if I haven’t died yet and been carried away by*Dupade*. On the other hand, maybe*Dupade*won’t come for me yet, maybe he won’t give death to my body, then I will teach you the parts of*Dupade*’s*Words*that are complex and difficult.

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*D**ajeguéo*.
english: Their land was ‘*dajegueo*’. Their chiefs were:*Guebei,*and*Cajodáquide*.

ayoreo: Jnusina dajai uje Ayoré. Uásejna ore tagu yacorenie ga chosõripise dajai mu que iguiotique cuse ga ore gajnepíode garósi iji yacore. Chajire dajai. Chojninga: “Yajire bora póroro mu que gajnepítique cuse ga yosõripis.”
english: She remembers*Jonoiné*for*Jonoiné*really killed jaguars, non-Ayoreos, and Ayoreos, and he told the truth. He was like the “south winds!” He had many, many victims.

ayoreo: Chi guei ca tagu uté ore jutícabia to. Ga jno ga tibágui nero, mu chi chise nero abai ujuya gajnei gase ga chi chojninga: “Ujuya yoc iji yoquidai. Uje guérique que tagu yoc!”
english: *Dachapiboraté*’s wife’s brother said: “I’m going to go to see my sister and her family.”

ayoreo: Chi di ji degúi, ga chojningame dacoté: “A yijína cuchátique.” Chiraja uje jotábia dési ijoyade gai te.
english: To get enough water Falling Rain was wringing the hair that grows right by his ear into his gourd cup and it filled with water, but there was a river plant floating on top.

ayoreo: Ujñarone u ore uruode ga chubúchu ga tṍraji. Daijnane ore chigase ga chijnímiiji posode ayoré. Daijna cuchábode ore. Ore iquetaque posode ayoré; ore chijnímiji ore gu. Pujopie garani dajnu ditáji ome jnacare bajade uje daijna ore. Ga ore tocade ga pujopie chiqueta raja ore iji dajnu ditáji ga daijna ore ga chijnímiji ore.
english: Ayoré persons who understand witchcraft know what the antidotes are and they can heal people who have been bitten by snakes. Breath, that is their word for blowing, and medicine comes out of them when they blow.

ayoreo: Chiese ore apose gatéode ga ca guérique tagu ayorérique.
english: And then they heard the eerie sound approaching and they said: “¡Set a barricade on fire!”

ayoreo:
Enga adi jec
u uje que yacágoja*Dupade*iguíjnai
tudé bisideque to enga ca yoco yoquichorája*Dupade*iguíjnai.*Dupade*déji danguresórone
gari, ga que ore doi ga ore chujnieta, aníri, dajñiequégaja ajei. Que ore doi
ga ore cana iji ajei. Que ore doi ga ore chimíñimi aja*Dupade*iguíjnai ajei.
english: And Moses feared*Dupade*and quickly took off his shoes. He didn’t ignore when*Dupade*said, ‘Don’t come near me until you take off your shoes, for the place where I am, is holy.’

ayoreo: Yacaranguipise ome guidáyode iji erámone uje Dupade Uruode déji guidáyode jnese iji erámone. Yacaranguipise ome guidáyode iji erámone. Dupade uruode déji Tobité, déji Pailón rĩ, déji don Len idai, déji Zapocó rĩ, déji Santa Cruz uéchade rĩ, déji ducuniri uñane, déji Paraguay iji queyéchade, déji Pajeidé idai. Yacaranguipise ome udojo guidáyode uje Dupade Uruode e déji guidáyode ga cojñone, ayoréode chingo yoque Dupade Uruode aja yoque.
english: Dupade – GodDupade’s Words – God’s WordAyoréos – Ayoré peopleCojñone – Non-Ayoré people

ayoreo: Chi jno uté, cojñoque jmatai. Tibágui eduguéjnai enga utaite iji quedéjnai. Chi ajéode u ujeta tagu potígo irérigui. Mu datéringa uje chetaque. Que áyipierigui déji Dupade uruode gotique gu.
english: Just as the rich man didn’t want to listen to*Dupade*’s*Word*, neither did Pajei and them. They tried to discourage Davidé and they said: “There’s nothing to*Dupade*’s*Word.*Now, women, that’s who I worship.”

ayoreo:
Yugúsi Tobité ga
yirasique uje yicatecã́ri Dupade ome uaque iji ne Rincón. Yicatecã́ri ome
ayoréode iji erámi jnese. Jé u Zapocógosóde ore.
english: **Dupade****’s Word****:** As if I really knew all about*Dupade*’s written*Word*, so, I’ll just teach you a little bit.

ayoreo:
Señora Marina, bajneque
yuruode, uje yipota ujetiga araja yipesudode cucha gajnarécho iji aníri, yuniri
udé. Iyajmánguipíse uje ujnusina anírone, yipesudode cucha gajnárecho. Ga
yipota ujetiga yibo, mu ujnusina cuchade uje gajnárecho iji yuniri, ga yipota
ujetiga yibo. Mu Dupade Uruode u ujetiga yibópise uje yacate ore ome Dupade
uruode nanique enga yátaja ua to uje bajnerame udore ome yocuruode.
english: *Señora Marina (Maxine Morarie)*, these words of mine are for you, because I want you to know the ugly things I have done here where I now live. For a long time I’ve been thinking about all the things I’ve done. I am very sad when I think about how bad things are here.

ayoreo: Mu qué adi jé u cojñoque bajade uje cojñone chi ore chotigase. Adi u uje yotigágo Meráminé, Juan ore. Uje yojníjo: “Arapataque Meráminé ore.”
english: But some of the first*cojñone*that we had contact with were unbelievers. And that is why we doubted the things that Meráminé, and Juan told us. And we said: “Meráminé and them are liars.”

ayoreo: E ore jno ga ore basiji ta, mu e yicopo rĩ. E ore chise degúi iji te bajade. Enga*Garáijnainái*chachai yu rĩ, ga yisíome yi ome ga chijnoc. Degu te. Jé u gajnerĩ uje yijíñongome yoquigode gajnerĩ iji diri.
english: And then we returned to our campsite.  And everyone was out getting wild pineapple food called*doridie.*Some of us saw the*orojno*bee. They said: “Wait for us to get the honey, and then we’ll leave when the sun is over there and the next place we come to will be our camp. And we’ll be carrying our salt.”

ayoreo: Que Picanerane que ore chejna dajénique aja guetique iji darurui gai.
english: But not the Picanerane. They didn’t have to work themselves up to kill victims.

ayoreo: Jnacari chingome yu uje pota*Maria*. Chojninga: “*Manuelné*, ango*Maria*ga yacoté jne.”
english: There was a young man who wanted to make love to*Maria*. He said to*Manuelné*: “*Manuelné,*tell*Maria*to be my wife.”

ayoreo: Yacaranguipis ome ua ome chequedíe ujetiga átapísaja ore gajnerĩ enga ca ore chimo pajei dotáningárique ome ñane gajnerĩ, yoque*Dupade*.
english: I’m very thankful to you tonight that, as you said you’d do, you are watching over all of us right now. Not only for your believers do I pray to you, but for all of us in our village so that some of them make the decision to accept you. Forgive their sins,*Yoqui-Dupade*, here in this village. I would be sad if we left our countrymen some of them here on earth when you come back again,*Yoqui-Dupade*. So, I’m talking to you about them that their hearts will be open to your words, and that they will present themselves to you and ask you to erase their sins.

ayoreo: Yirasique yu uje ore chujé tãrara ga cho ujetiga cho disiode uje yoquirasique yocabode yui uje yajnángoji data. Yirasique yui uje ore chujé tãrara. Yisárase cuchade mu yojninga, ‘E yiya jne.’ Yiya ga yajnai to jne Dupade iguíjnai udé. Chãje yirasique yui gu. Gajnui u disiode uje yajnángoji datá ga yoquirasique udé Dupade iguíjnai.
english: I’m happy when they ring the bell for church, and it’s like the kind of happiness we felt when we were children and went about with our friends. I look forward to them ringing the bell. I am about to start doing something, but when I hear the bell, I say, I’m going to do this later. I stop what I’m doing and take off for God’s house. It’s because I’ve been happily looking forward to it. I’m like a child getting together with my friends, that’s how happy we are to be going to church.

ayoreo: *Duábidácabi*chi chĩ́cara ore iji dajei. Chi chojninga: “Yĩ́cara ga cho daijnai*Ajópedé*.” Enga ore chedu ujetiga ore chujé. Chi ore déji yoquiquigade*(dialecto diferente: queyiquigade).*Jé*Ajópedái*chiraja uje ore déji yoquiquigade enga ore chujé yoque dirome. Mu chaca*Ajódedái*iji ta. Chajna cutérone iji degújnai*(el campamento que tenía ayer)*. Chicai te. E yajnángo dajei. Jé chaca. Chicai te. Mu yico degúi enga yicoi doridie.
english: But U*ayé*was lying. She was a liar. We returned because she was lying about the fight going on. And she said: “*Ajópedé*bawled you guys out, and now they are turning on*Ajópedái*and about to come to blows.

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi á*, a angureta jóroque ua ome yajnatígo, yatígo ore to durasedie. ¿Que ajna gójni a? babárique iji ore. Urénga cuchiyíode jõrai tupúsu dajeque toguéodedatéi, dajeque tuguréodedatéi, yajnamenie ore dai tuqué,*Dejabi*uqué gajneri. Yacãra agoyé uneréjnaja tudaque ome*Cucha Urõsódatéi*uqué.
english: *Oh, you who caused such awful harm*, in vain was your confidence in being able to kill my grandsons and my sons. How is it that you haven’t, as you planned, been able to take one of them as your victim?

ayoreo: Jecute yingongo uaque gajnerĩ uje Jesuse u yoquijõrai enga jé basa yocai u Dayé to, Dupade. Jecute yingongo ñane gajnerĩ uje Jesuse chi gajnui tu yocatoi, uje yocadi chichagáraja ñane iji. Enga uje a Jesuse di ome yoque, enga a yamátago yogaja Jesuse, yocuráguedie aja gaté uje jno yocúdi, yoque uje Jesus cho daoi iji yoque enga yoco yocatoi iji uté. Jecute Pablo chingo yoque unojo dacajnaquéode ujetiga yacaco ñane ome Jesuse adode to. Enga yicoi Dupade iroquéode to gajnerĩ.
english: Paul knew*Dupade*and that’s why he reached out to others. And in the same way, we do good things for others because we know*Dupade*; and we teach each other about his Word. And we serve*Dupade*by doing good things for each other. When we teach each other about the things of the Lord,*Dupade*notices what we are doing. He knows that we don’t know everything about his Word and that we need to be taught, and that is why He himself is pleased to teach us through his Holy Spirit.

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “¿Cuchárique guejnaque báningo?” Ga chojninga: “Gajnoque Quedéjna chejna yayode ore.”
english: They say he ran off and climbed a **chúrume*i tree. (A small tree with thorns.) And he said: “This kind of tree (with thorns) is not found by the river.” They say he slept in the branches of the ‘*chúrumei’*tree all night long. At daylight he left.

ayoreo:
Gosode a
chojninga: “Tac ua, jecute Dupade ujnacari que di.”
english: Some might say: “You’re lying, Jesus is not going to return.”

ayoreo:
Jé uacadúcho uje chi qué ocoi
menenéngone, cojñone ore iji erámi uje que ore chudute Dupade Uruode, mu e
yugúcoi ore gatócoro. Jecute Jesuse ojninga casodicaite, gãrapie uñanie e
tocade.
english: You have heard that there are yet many*menenéngone*(people like the*Ayoré*), and other people in the world that haven’t heard*Dupade*’s words, and at one time the*Ayoré*were among them. Therefore, some of the sayings of Jesus concerning the signs of his arrival have already taken place, because now the*Ayoré*have heard.

ayoreo: Ga chi ore doi*Tináiné*, ga*Tináiné*chijna re aja jogadi, ore ijnanguiéni, ga chojninga: ‘Déji de uacajnanguiéni.’
english: And when they brought Tináiné, Tináiné would take himself right to where the lost thing was.

ayoreo:
Mu Jesus
ojningane unojo que piyaque gotique. Ayoré yoque ga obiréjna yocuruode quigode.
Obiréjna Jesus acajnaquéode iji yocuruode quigode. Mu yoque gosi jeta ca
changureta Dupade to jnenga, jeta apota angureta to, enga piotiguei qué u
deréjnai unojo jeta ojninga: “Yingo yu aja ua, Jesus á, enga ajnoque yate
gajnaréngone enga ṍra ore aja yote quedéjnai údi enga jé u ujetiga yijína banguresórone
ore to jne jeta ãri.”
english: But he would say this to those who are in*the lake of fire**:*“But if people hate me, they will follow you. They will find themselves in*the lake of fire.*But if they accept what I’ve done for them and put their faith in me, they won’t go where you are.” This is what he would say. “In vain you want people to come to me.” This is what*Dupade*would say to them.

ayoreo: E ico ga ibácoi ta. Enga icopo rĩ ga yocatigai uné. Ga e yicoi cuchade iji dati, chicorone. Mu imo cutẽri iji ebédua enga*Garáijnaidái*chijno. Ga yipéco.
english: And then we left and slept again. At daylight we left again and*what’s-his-name*said,*Pongora dáyabí*said: “Come on, we’ll stop later. And then the next time we stop, we’ll eat.”

ayoreo: *(Oidábiadé*cho quique: Mu que ore chiraja nanique uje a Satanas utocaidode u nanique, uje ore chiroque yocáquiode ore aja dáringaringo pocaningai gaisóño.)
english: But some, even thought they didn’t breath in and out after eating*ají*, proved to be greater warriors than some who did suck their breath in and out, because when they went into battle their enemies couldn’t overcome them.

ayoreo:
Jé yajéo gu, uje ise mu ca tague
yu go ome yadode udojo. E gusu udojo yadode uje yicárasiji Santa Cruz to
nanique. Anire, ise mu ca tague yu go uje yabayode gare. Yiya nanique gu uje
anire pota gapudíe jiedie. Yojninga: ‘Yiya to jne.’ Ga yijína Jnani ga e gusu.
Que toque jeta yijína jnacare uñeque.
english: That’s what I wanted to do, and I don’t think I lied about myself or about what I did. The only thing I did was to go to Santa Cruz. And I think I’ve not lied about having had only two husbands. I only left my first husband because he’d been unfaithful with many young women. And I told myself, ‘I will leave him.’ And then, I went to*Jnani*and only him. There is no way I would go to any other man.

ayoreo: Mu yocacaranguipise ome Dupade uje jé yijnángo Dupade ẽracáidode cucha ome yoque uje Dupade que chachía cuchade gotique iji dapesudi uyoque ayoré. Socaseti cho darúja yoque iji anire, uje Espíritu Sánto di.  Espíritu Sánto chi di jne enga chojninga: “Espíritu Sánto u ujeta chátaja uaque ga chisi ome uaque pirajatiguei, pirajatiguedatérique.”
english: Though our knowledge is little, it’s only right that we teach you because Dupade’s words say that even though we are few, Dupade is in our presence. Even though our understanding is limited, we give to our fellow people what we do understand, and the little becomes much. Dupade does not want us to ignore meetings where his words are taught, because when we get together to hear his words he gives wisdom to us. He gave us our lips, our heads, our understanding, our hearing, our hands, our strength, our joy. And he gave us these good things to use when we share his Word. He didn’t give them to us without a purpose. But Satan is also here among us to erase Dupade’s words.

ayoreo:
Mu chi Eba chi chojnira: “Que
yagu gotique.”
english: But Eve said: “I won’t eat it!”

ayoreo: Jecucha ore naráraja maquina mu que yicháji yuruode aja to go iji de máquina iji iguíjnai iji Dupade iguíjnai. Mu yicháji to iji de. Yiraja yu gu ga jé yicháji to yuruode. Anire, jecucha yojninga: “¿Jé gotique gu a uje que yicháji yurúrigo to?”
english: People are always recording their words, but I never have until now put my words on the machine here in God’s house. Right now, I’m putting them on it. I am sure of my faith and that is why I’m doing it. I’m always telling myself: “Why is it I never have told about my faith and put it on the machine?”

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*G**uerácadie*, ga jiei ore to.
english: *Cuterepajógosode*or*Cuterepaogorónegosode –*“People of honey” (called by two names) Their land was referred to as*‘guerácadie’*; there were many*Cutérepajógosode*.

ayoreo:
Mu a Jesuse,
Dupade, chajnai ga chojninga, “Mu bei que déji darujna aone to go. Que yiji
orúdi to uje que ore i déji darujna aone go. Yiraja uje ore ijója yuruode ga
ore tangári. Ore chirate yiradedie ga ore chiraja quigode. Ore chiraja uje ore
catecã́ri yu, mu que ore changureta yu. Que yurúsãre ore i iji gaté ga que
yiji ore údi gotique,” chojninga.*(Efesios
2:8 aja 9)* *Ecarai – Tobité, Bolivia**–**1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ecarai acajnaquéode udojo*
english: Now when he returns, it will be a time of sorrow again, but also, for us who believe, a time of happiness. It will be a happy time for the Ayoreos, and the rest of us when we leave with Jesus. And they will rejoice with Jesus as they near his city in heaven. But their relatives who do not believe in Jesus will not leave for Jesus’s big city in heaven. People will miss each other, in vain. They will miss brothers and sisters. Jesus’s return will be like it was when he was killed in his human body and the earth dwellers rejoiced long ago as they finished him off. But on the other hand, through his death Jesus brought his believers into salvation, and took them up to heaven with him and their unbelieving relatives will miss them with no hope of seeing them again.

ayoreo: Chi ca chatata go,*Dachagaidé*. Que chatata dei go. Chi ore po, dajoguenie, dai ejode. Ijoguenie chi ore po ejode.
english: The oldest son said: “Probably*Dachagaidé*wanted us to think the grave was somewhere else.” He said, “This place is too neat with it’s mound. This is probably where he buried him, that’s why he worked so hard to camouflage it.*Dachagaidé*thought he’d found a place that wouldn’t be discovered. And it’s right here by the road,” he said, “Surely this is it.”

ayoreo: *Oidabiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: He was inside the house with his companions, planning how to kill*Dachapiboraté*. He said: “I’ll kill that witch doctor*Dachapiboraté*who eats from his garden.”

ayoreo:
Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipis ome
ua uje barote yóguiji erámone diríca iji yocajesúode erámio. Yugúcopóji de rĩ
jnese, jnanione ore uyoque. Yoque Dupade, jeta goto déji siñeque, jetiga cáma
unere baruode yui tome to gajneri, ga asíome gajneri baruode, ga unere yui ome
to gajneri, yoque Dupade. Gusu ua uje baruode cho cucha uneréjnane ome yoque,
Dupade.
english: Our Dupade, we pray to you today at our Friday meeting. I thank you, and we are happy that we are going to hear your Word today. Our Dupade, I am not ashamed to share your Word with my companions who are true believers. I am not ashamed of your Word even when others laugh at us.

ayoreo: Chi ore chujé ga chi Chiquenone ore chujé bajaque. Chi ore chijna dajéode aja guesi tiji gai. Ga chi ayoré dacasutedie ipesungórone u udojo, ome*Jaboidé*. Chiquenone chi ore chijna dajéode aja guesi ga ore cho je-je-je iji gai.
english: Once long ago, the Ayoreos ingested red hot peppers, and it made them so anger inside  that they ended up killing a very ugly official, according to*Jaboidé*.

ayoreo:
Enga arajáyo gotique, yujodie
chequei uaque, uje yirasé yu, mu que yirasepise yu go. Arajayope gotique,
yujodie chequei, uje yirasé yu mu que yirasepise yu go. Ujeta jec ũré ga
arajáyo Dupade uruode. Yirasé yu gu anire, yirasé yu mu que yirasepise yu
gotique; a jé ca déji uaque to. Uje yirasé yu mu uje yangureta Dupade ome
yajéode. Mu aníre, u yabai uje cáma changureta Dupade to, ga u uje a que
yirasepise yu go. Uerate jetiga yejnángo yógaja Dupade Uruode – yabai, yabode uyoque.
english: I love it when my fellow women stand before us and give their testimonies. I don’t know if you who are my fellow believers enjoy this also, but I do. I like to hear the stories they tell about themselves. I’m also happy that we are increasing in number in our meetings.

ayoreo: Yocacaranguipísome Dupade uje aníri, Ipeyai ore déji yoque, enga ore chingo yoque ome Dupade. Dupade anguresõre uaque, jé ca yajéjome queyate gajnaréngone ome cucha anínique gotique. Gajnárec jeta yãrángome darai tiji yipajei. Ga adi u Dupade jeta chajéngome ore to jeta di.
english: If we stray from Dupade’s ways, we dishonor*Dupade*, and his beauty is not seen in us. If we stray it’s as though we are dead to*Dupade*’s ways. It’s also as though we are ignorant to the things of Dupade. It’s as though we knew nothing about him. Therefore, I want to tell you about how I used to live.

ayoreo: Jnacarode ore di ijibujaidasorane ga ore chucué dai. Mu ore chopise dai casica uje ore abujadie yorícojna uje umare. Ore peropíse dai. Ore daté chojninga: “Aguitóyo. Yijnájoi uacai casé.*Dachagaidé*jõcaratáguebei a chujé.”
english: He quickly buried his victim, to hide what he had done. He put sticks over the top of the grave to make it look less like a grave.

ayoreo: Cho cuyuque dejai gatócoro mu ayorei chisa yu. Chojninga: “Yimóji bari jne.” Yo cuyuc mu ayorei chisa yu. Yajire mu yajei u ayoré quedéjna mu yiguios. Mu yojninga: “Bo, ajni to. Que yipota ua.”
english: It was the middle of the night when someone grabbed me. He said: “I’m going to sleep with you.” I awoke and someone grabbed me. I looked and thought it was a stranger but it was a relative. But I said: “Go away. I don’t want you.”

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Ga gajnui jé u. Mu chi quigade ga uqué jno, jnani atai. Chi jnopo rĩ ga chicai uté.
english: And you know that your teachers with their knowledge have told you how later the rich man was sorry. He went to Abraham but had to go back to the*lake of fire.* That’s how it was with an encounter that don Len had with a man on the train. This man hated everything. Len didn’t know why this was so. The man said to him: “In vain I went to one of the churches where you believers in*Dupade*go. I went in, and we were with them, but it didn’t help me at all.”

ayoreo: ¡Chi chise ode! Chajire mu jnup umare aja.
english: The oldest son said to the youngest: “You go to one of the villages. I’ll go to the other villages where our father was going when he left us. Surely someone has killed him,” that’s what he said to his younger brother.

ayoreo: Chi e iyangóri di. I tu ‘*Carujnángue Erusṍri*’. Chojninga: “Ajoso da ti. Yise gajnerĩ ga yacoté jne.”
english: *Corabé*would say to them: “Hurry up. The person who reaches me first will be my husband right now.” She reached her hand down to help them up. She would say: “Take my hand, take my hand!” But they would get just so far up and then slip down again; they would slip down to the ground.

ayoreo: “¡E chijna!”
english: When a person is ‘touched’ they say,*He got him!*

ayoreo: Jnanione ore dayode u iyatade. Iyatade ore narare ore gai uje ore chajna guesi iji taningapísi. Ore chojninga: “Ãrijíño ga ajnáño uaté aja guesi ga amácho to jne,  ga yoco paraque to jne.”
english: The men who make preparations for the Bird Festival are called ‘*Iyatade’;*only grown men and their fathers participate in being ‘*Iyatade.’*It’s the “Iyatade” that speak over the young men participants and get them going in the very beginning of the Festival. They say: “Wake up! Follow her! Chase after her! And when you do it, we’ll be very quiet.”

ayoreo: –ore uniri tu*C**ucójnai*.
english: There were large*cucoi*trees in their land, but they left there and began to live in the ‘*jachai*’  and in the plains. Their chiefs were:*Jonanine,*and*Acui*.

ayoreo:
**Dupade urásapísi doi jnumi jeta cháta raja Jesus anguretasórone:***(Juan 14:16)*Dupade chojninga: “Yujnacarabi Jesuse di iji yu, mu a yirógape yurasade uñéngaja ore jne,” chojninga, uje chamáñaje chijnóra yoque gu.

Uje uacamóño uje Jesuse chi
jnome erámi nanique uje jnoji jnumi nanique, ga jnome gatajnoquei ga chacája
guei abujadie. Ga gusu yóguiji jnumi. Que qué Dupade uñeque dépoji yocajei.

Mu
Jesuse chojningame danguresórone: “A yiji to jne. Yicai yiapade to jne. Mu que yipota jeta disijate
uaque iji yiquigade. Enga a yiroque yurasade uñéngaja jnumi jne. Yapade
irótique enga jeta u Docosõrasṍri. (Docosõrasṍri tu uje cojñone ore
chojningame*Consolador*. Jé basa
ayoréode yocuruode qui tu uje ayoréode chojningame*posõrasṍri*.)

Ga chojninga: “Yirógaja jnumi
jne,” chojninga. “Enga a jecucha chaji uacari jne, dirode, dejade ore,” chojninga.

Dupade charase jnani bajade ore, mu chosõre yoque ga chiroque dajnacari Jesuse aja jnumi te rĩ. Mu ujnacari Jesuse jnome gatajnoquei nanique ga jé chirogapója yoque darasade uñai taja jnumi te rĩ. Enga i tu*Posõrasṍri*. Enga uacarajáyo uje unojo Dupade osõraquéode unojo ocoi ome yoque. Yojnicopo aja uté*Dupade Ujopié Espiritu Santo*rĩ enga yocosõrasṍri jé u uté iji dejade, dirode ore jnese. Ga chátaja yoque uje yoco yocáyugu ome cuchade, enga chisome dajopié ome yoque to jeta etoque yoc.

**Ecarai chacãra dacajnaquéode aja gosi úringárique cucherãi:**Ayorérique, chequéraque ¿déji jeta ca chosóre datique? Uje chamáñaje ape gu, ujnacare gu. Uaque gosi changureta Jesuse, mu gosi ujetiga cha Dupade úringárique pangurete, mu que toc jeta uaque gosi úringárique cucherãi íjnonguiji Dupade. Úringai ayoré panguresṍri ome Dupade a qué déji. Jé uacamóño uje a ayorérique déji siñec, uaque gosi a ujnacanique a déji tóniñeque, mu uaque gosi úringai cucherãi que íjnoque iji gotique. Chamáñaje jé ape gu.

Ga adi jec u uaque gosi. A gosi
cha Dupade mu uaque gosi úringai panguretasṍri que jnoji Dupade gai gotique
iji Dupade idai. A yicai tõniñeque. A yajnico yoque nanique mu yocúringai, yocúringa
casicai cucherác que jnoji yu gotique. Chamáñaje ore ujnacare yu gu. Jé ore ape
yu.

Jé basája ga a yabi David a
jnosi. Chicási tõniñeque enga toi, enga chicai punire quedéjnai. Uje yijócãre
uje angoroneparigape. Mu que ayorérique déji siñeque ujeta chojninga: “Bape
garãte u que, que etógapo ome ua gotique rĩ. Cucherãi unejángome ua.”

Enga jé adi u uje a yicai
tõniñeque ome Dupade, mu Dupade ujnacare yu, ga que úringai cuchẽrac
íjnonguiji yu gotique. Que Dupade cha yu gotique. Que cha timíninga yu aja
quedéjnane. Mu uyu u uje yicai quedéjnane ome Dupade.

Enga unojo u uje uacarajáyo uje
que toc jetiga ayorérique írique, anguretaretigai, íjnonguiji Dupade go. Dupade
ijnoraquei que íjnoguiji ua go uje chijnóra ua. Jé basája David diosi ga
yiraja yujnacarape ga, ujetiga jnacarape ga, que yirajápo gotique rĩ. Mu
yiguiosode, yujo cucherane u uje ore chiraja. Enga que toc ujetiga íjnoque
úringai cucherãc. Enga adi jec u ome Dupade.

**Que toc jeta Dupade cha danguretasórone uyoque:**Dupade que toc jeta cha yoc. A yiya Dupade iroquéode. Que bangureta Dupade mu que cha ua to go. A aya Dupade adode gajnerĩ enga banguíra pajegátigai taja Dupade Ujopié tiji bajei. Yirajágo uje ayorérique a pojna daguiotique, daguiotóraque. A cho dáyugu re ga ajéngome daguiotodie jeta tibágapoi jeta taquirapoi ore to rĩ. Ga adi jec u uje baya Dupade adode, ga Dupade Ujopie chajo raja anire,*pajei ajidé*ga cho dáyugu re. Tajíja re. (Hunch shoulders from sadness).

“Mu gotique uje cha yu,” chojninga. Tajíja re iji bajei
ajidé uje chajéngome ua, bapesudode cucha gajnarécho. Mu adi jec u. Que Dupade
Ujopie íjnonguiji bajei gotique uje chamáñaje Dupade ujnacare ua gu. Tajíja re
iji bajei ajidé enga cho dáyugu re, mu que íjnonguiji ajei gotique Dupade
Ujopie uje ore chojningame*Espíritu Santo*.
Que íjnoque iji bajei gotique.
Jé cháji bajei ajidé. Cho dáyugu re iji. Baya Dupade. Babei erámi jogonórone
cucha. Mu Dupade Ujopié que íjnonguiji bajei gotique. Chamáñaje Dupade ujnacare
ua gu. Que toc jeta qué anoningase Dupade Ujopié iji bajei. Mu cho dáyugu re ga
tajíja usodi bajei ajidéraque.

**Yoquipotigué ayoréode, Dupade Ujopié Espíritu Sánto, chetaque ñane iji yocajéode:***(Gálatas 5:13,16,17)*Uacarajáyo uje Dupade chijnóra yoque, ga ojningai tuné déji yocari iji de jnumi tuné. Uacarajáyo to uje Dupade chijnóra danguresórone enga ajéode u ujeta yiquérango iji adode enga yicoi iroquéode. Mu uacarajáyo uje ayoré yoc ga yicoi yocúringai ayoré aoi te ga erámi aoi te ga, pipotiguéode isai te ga, erámi jogonórone isatodie enga yoquedodie u uje chimo erámi. Yocaoi tu uje pota erámi. Chamáña gu uje ugúcoi erámi jogonóri pao gu.

Jé basája ga Dupade chi di jne
enga na yógaja yocao ichai uje que tãratápo gotique rĩ. Enga unojo u uje que qué
pipotiguérique, puru patáringa chisapo yóguiji ne jnumi rĩ.

Enga ajnusináño uje bangureta Dupade enga ajnusina uje
que qué bájningarãpója ore gotique rĩ.

Jesute, Dupade, Espiritu Santo
ore iguíjnai tu ua enga jé ca bepo báyipie jeta ojninga: “Yi jne enga yójiape
yijódi to jne. Yi jne enga aníre, yo yurupatáringa jne. Yi jne enga yipojna
yujórique ayoré.”

Mu baoi jnuteminóri ipotiguéode
u unojo uje pota erámi jogonórone cucha. Mu yacaranguipis ome Dupade uje Dupade
chijnóra yoque, ga chiroque dajnacari Jesuse aja jnumi. Mu ujnacari Jesuse
jnome gatajnoquei ga chirogape uñai aja jnumi te rĩ, enga unojo u uje qué déji
yocari iji deréjnane unojo jnese.

**Jesus chi di jne enga jno yocúdi aja gaté:**Mu a chimo yoque enga jeta ugúcoi erámi jnese enga Dupade anguretasórone uyoque u uje jno yocúdi. Gosi ujetiga pota nóna yoque to jnenga, gosi changuretape Jesuse. Jesuse qué chijna yibijnani uje qué ugúcoji jnumi. Uje Jesuse chi di jne enga gajnui tu jeta goto edo simata ga edabi sudé déji dajogadi te rĩ.

Ga gosi a ca chojninga jne: “¡Ácayope
ga yijína uaque to ée! ¡Ité áa! ¡Ácarape, yijína uaque to ée! ¡Yapáa! ¡Ácarape
ga yijína uaque to ée!”

Mu Jesuse que uruode u gotique.
Chojninga: “Piotiguéode qué u uje yiroque yirógode ore aja erámi,” chojninga.
“Ujetiga ore chingo ore jne uje a yo yujuñame erámi te jne,” chojninga.

Jé uacamóño uje jnani bajade,
Jesuse chiroque ore uje ore chojningame*proféta*.*Proféta*udore u uje ore chatata Dupade
Uruode bajaque aja ore iquéode. Chirógaja ore nanique, mu que ore tangári
anírone gotique, ore acajnaquéode. Ga uyoque yocajnúone jé u*profétade*ore uje yingongo Dupade
Uruode aja ore, yocujode ayoréo iji ne jnumi. Enga ujetiga apota ajína yoque to
jnenga, ango Dupade ujeta chĩro basijnaringuéone to. Enga na dajóningai taja
ua to. Na pajei sereringai taja ua to. Na pijnoraquei taja ua to ujetiga apota
ajína yoque to aja gatajnoquei.

**Ayorérique toi ga pujnusiétigadaté jogadi tu ome daguiosode:**Yiraja uje ayorérique gotique a toi enga pujnusiétigadaté. Chamáñaje ore iguiosi cho jmíti-jmíti ome ore gu. Ga adi jé u uje Dupade a di jne enga piguiosode uñéngo ujetiga ca ore changureta to Dupade enga, a pujnusiétigai jogadi tu jne. Pujnusiétigai jogadi mu que toc ujeta qué pimongapo yocúringárique yoquiguiotique jne rĩ. Uacamóño ujeta gosode toi enga que yimóngopo ore gotique rĩ.

**Jesus a chiringare yocai casodicai ore enga a chucué ore íode iji degode darujna ao:**Mu uje chi Jesuse di iji jnumi tudé enga qué chiringare yocai casodicai jnani bajade ore. Gaidi tu ujetiga qué chinóninga ore íringo. Jetiga ore uñéngo íringo déji daruja aode, pucuecaringuei gajnesórone to. Mu que ore déji ga pujnusiétigai jogadi e u ome yoc.

Jecute Jesuse chojningame
darógode ore: “Acayoi erámi jnese,” chojninga. “Mu jé ca ajnócho
uacaguidéraque gotique,” chojninga, “cachi uacarídade,” chojninga. “Jé ca
ajnáño uacaplátaraque aja erámi gotique jeta boyo Dupade uruode údi,”
chojninga. “Chamáñaje uacangureni tu yu,” chojninga. (*Mateo 28:18-20)*

Enga unojo u uje yirajágo
ujetiga apótayo ajína yoque to jnenga, ango yocai Dupade to. Ga Dupade chĩrope
basijnaringuéone. Enga anguretape Dupade uje qué ingome ua aja uje qué eca ua.
Jé basa ga a yitoi jne enga ore déji, Dupade anguresórone, mu ore uruode qué u.
Jé socaseti a toji gosi acajnaquérigo yui.

Mu yiraja uje ayoréode yocajneque pitoringai enga que yocuñeque déji siñeque jeta ca toi to jne. Gosi a chise deruei te jne, mu eruei tupúsu, ga eruei tupúsu uje que chimopo pucuecaringuérique uñeque rĩ.

Jecute Dupade uruode chojninga,
uje Jesuse chojninga: “Gosi uje changureta yu ga,” chojninga, “yisíome
pucuecaringuei ome. Pucuecaringueque ichai uje eyúgojma. Enga que eruénique
cusapo gotique rĩ,” chojninga.

Yiraja uje jnacare ua enga
barasique ua, ga chi unere anírone jeta basõre ome dosapéode, unere ujeta
baque, ujeta anganare gapudie, unere ujetiga ajo bejnai. Enga bajos eti ca beruénique
cus. Mu que yimóngo Dupade diringai.

Yiraja uje gapu ua ga barasé ua.
Bangana erámone enga bajos eti jé bate jne, mu jé bate ujetiga apota Dupade.
Ujetiga apota Dupade úringárique pangurete ga badi a jé u iji deréjnane
gatuaque ujetiga angureta Dupade.

Jé basa ga jé a ijõre ua, mu a Dupade
di jnenga cho dajuñame ua uje jé bajõrai tu Satanase. Bagaidi tu Satanase
áyipiedie jnenga, báringai ayoré jnusiétaque jogadi tu iji Dupade guetei
punire. Jecute yingome ua. Ujéta cáma angureta Dupade to enga anguretape uje
qué piotiguei déji.

Jé uacadúcho to uje a Jesute
diapo jne rĩ enga que qué piotiguéode déji to iji siñeque rĩ. Adi jé u
ujetiga ayorérique chingo dojñaringo aja dojórique ayoré ga bangári. Chamáñaje
piotiguei u gu uje bamo ojñasṍri. Unojo Dupade uruode ujetiga apota ga
angureta Dupade enga anguretape. Jeoga iji dejai, jeoga iji dirome jne. Uje
cáma yico. ¡Jeta yojningai u yu gotique jeta cáma yi! mu yojningai tu Dupade
diringai uje Dupade di jnenga. Ujeta cáma di dirome enga cáma yico gotique, mu
jé upo yu to rĩ uje cáma yi go. A yico to dirome iji*domíngo*jeoga iji*miércoles.*A uacarajáyo*miércoles.*Mu cáma yico
to. Que yimo jeta cáma yico ome Dupade idai. Gusu yacajnaquéode ome yoque. Ga
yicatecã́ri Dupade ome yoque.

**Ecarai catecã́ri Dupade:**Yoque Dupade, yicatecã́ri ua iji gajnerĩ iji dejai udé uje yacaranguipis ome ua uje badute yoquicatecãraitigade, yoque Dupade. Uje yicatecãcoi ua diríca ujetiga ójnepe guéode yoquibai diríca, yoque Dupade, jecute yacãre babai gajnerĩ ome baramurajane guéo. Ujetiga jnanione ore irasique dacade cucha gajnerĩ. Ujetiga ijóño yoque, yoque Dupade, enga ore catecã́ri ua gajnerĩ, enga ore chacãre babai to gajnerĩ uje bajnóra yoque gu. Uje bamate guéode yoquibai, yoque Dupade. Ga ore irasique dacade gajnerĩ enga ore tagu áriguiji gajnerĩ.

Enga jé adi tu gajnerĩ ujetiga ore irasique baruode
ujetiga ore tagu. Jecute yicatecã́ri ua gajnerĩ ujetiga arodipise yoque
gajnerĩ iji ne, Rincóngosode uyoque. Ujetiga átaja yoque gajnerĩ enga asíome
yoque pijoningai tome yoque gajnerĩ, yoque Dupade. Asíome petotiguei tome
yoque, gajnerĩ, yoque Dupade. Asíome pijnoraquei tome yoque. Uje yicatecã́ri
ua ome Jesus i. Amẽ.

*Ecarai – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1960**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ecarai acajnaquéode*
english:
*Dupade chiroque uñéngaja yoque jeta cháta raja yoque*

**Dupade****sent****another to****earth to help his Son’s believers: (John 14:16)**

*Dupade*said: “My dear son Jesus is back with me, but I will send another to help you,” he said this because he loved us.

You see, Jesus went away and left this earth long ago. He went back to heaven in the clouds. And we were alone on earth. No longer did we have any of*Dupade*within us.

But Jesus said to his believers: “I’m going to be leaving soon. I’m going back to my Father. But I don’t want you to be orphans after I leave. And I will send another of us to earth. My Father will send him, and he will be a*comforter.*(The*comforter*is what the people call*Consolador.*And in Ayoré we say*Posõrasṍri.)*

Jesus said: “I will send him to earth,” he said. “And he will be with you always, night and day.”

It’s as though*Dupade*stopped communicating with the first people that lived long ago, but he had compassion on us and sent his son Jesus to earth. But his son Jesus left for heaven long ago, and then it was that he sent another to us from heaven. And his name was “*Comforter.*” And, as you know,*Dupade*has shown us his comfort in many ways. We also call the one he sent “*Dupade**’s power the Holy Spirit**”*, and he is our comforter, and comforts and helps us day and night. He helps us when we are sad, and he gives us his power and strengthens us.

**Ecarai likens his teaching to clan names****:**

Is there an Ayoré man or woman that does not have compassion for his or her child? Of course not, it’s their child, their son. One of you that believes in Jesus, but later stops believing, it’s not possible that he will lose the clan name that his father*Dupade*gave him. His state of being a believer in*Dupade*stays with him. You know that even though someone’s son is far way from him, his clan name remains with his son and will never leave him at all. His son is still his son.

It’s the same with any one of you. He might stop believing in*Dupade*, but his state of being*Dupade*’s believer does not leave him at all, because it is intact in heaven. I could go far away. We might remove ourselves from our family, but the clan names we were given long ago would remain our clan names. I would still be their son. I would still be their child.

Let’s say my dear son David took off. He went far away to another country and it was as though he died. Or let’s say I kicked him out because he was disobedient. There’s nobody anywhere who would say: “The relationship you had with that child you kicked out is no longer strong. He has a different clan name now, instead of yours.”

In the same way, if I go far away from*Dupade*, I’m still*Dupade*’s son, and his clan name remains with me.*Dupade*will not abandon me at all. He will not stop watching over me wherever I am. But I am the one that goes away from*Dupade*.

And this is how you can know that it isn’t possible for a person’s name nor his faith to ever to taken away from*Dupade*. The love that*Dupade*has for you will never end.

Let’s say that David comes back, and I recognize that he is my dear son, and if I recognize him as my son, I will never disown him. But my relatives, those of my clan recognize him also. And it is not possible for him to lose his clan name. And that’s how it is with*Dupade*.

**It’s not possible for****Dupade****to****abandon us.**

It’s not possible for*Dupade*to abandon us. I can stop obeying his commands. You may stop believing in*Dupade*, but he won’t stop believing in you at all. You could stop following*Dupade*and make the Holy Spirit ashamed of you. We know how a man, or a woman sometimes scolds their relatives. And they feel embarrassed around their relatives and don’t visit them anymore. It’s much like that when you stop following*Dupade*.*Dupade*’s Holy Spirit stays in a corner of you heart and is very sad. He hunches his shoulders from sadness.

“Why has he abandoned me?”*Dupade*’s power, the Holy Spirit says. He is hunched over in the corner of your heart, ashamed of the sinful things you have done. That’s what happens. The Holy Spirit doesn’t leave your heart, for you are*Dupade*’s son. He is all hunched up in the corner of your heart, very sad, but he’s still there in your heart,*Dupade*’s Power that they call the Holy Spirit. He never leaves your heart at all. That’s because you are*Dupade*’s son. And it’s impossible for you to destroy the Holy Spirt that indwells you. But he is very sad and is all hunched up in that tiny corner of your heart.

**Our sinful nature and the Spirit of****Dupade****are constantly fighting each other inside of us:***(Galatians 5:13, 16, 17)*

You know that*Dupade*loves us, and the one he told us about is with us here on this earth. You also know that*Dupade*loves his believers and wants us to grow in our faith and to serve him. But you know we are human and we do what our human flesh wants to do, and it is controlled by the earthly part of us. Our earthly eyes see only the things of this world. Our flesh wants the world. And that is because we dwell in earthly bodies.

On the other hand,*Dupade*’s Son is coming back, and he will give us a new body, a body that will not rot. And then, never more will the lusts, nor the lying of our earthly bodies grab onto to us again. And remember that you believe in*Dupade*and remember that you’ll never be angry with anyone again.

Your body is a house for Jesus, and*Dupade*, and the Holy Spirit and so you must not do whatever you want. You must not say, ‘I’m going to get drunk, I’m going to lie about it, I’m going to scold that fellow-man of mine.”

Your earthly body wants to do those things, for it always wants to be like the world. But I thank*Dupade*, that*Dupade*loves us, and that he sent his son Jesus to this world. And then, when his son Jesus went back to heaven, he sent another to earth, and he will be with us forever.

**Jesus is coming back to take us to heaven****:**

But he will see us all over the world, and we who are*Dupade*’s believers, he will take us away to be with him forever. Whoever wants to go with us, must put his faith in Jesus. May Jesus use our lips while we are still in this world. For Jesus said when he comes back it will be quickly, like the blink of a little eye.

May no person say: “Wait, and I’ll go with you! Mother! Wait, and I’ll go with you! Father! Wait, I’ll go with you!”

But Jesus didn’t say these words. He said: “There’s still time for I am sending my servants into the whole world,” he said, “so that they will tell people that I’m going to punish this world.”

You may know about the men who lived long ago that were called prophets. The prophets were the ones that told about*Dupade*’s words concerning the future. They were sent by*Dupade*, but the people would not listen to their teaching. Those of us who tell*Dupade*’s word today to our fellow people are like those prophets. If you want to go with us when Jesus comes, ask*Dupade*to forgive your sins. If you do, he will put his health in you. He’ll put peace into your hearts. He’ll put love into your lives if you decide to go with us to heaven.

**When a person dies, there is much sorrow****among his relatives:**

We know that when a person, whoever he is, dies there’s great sorrowing because their relative has been separated from them. It will be like that when*Dupade*arrives, there will be relatives that will be very sad because they did not believe in*Dupade*. Despite their sadness, it won’t be possible for them to see us in our state of being a relative again. You know that we will never again see those who die.

**One day Jesus will awaken****our****forefathers****and look for their names in his Book:**

But when Jesus returns to earth, he will wake up our forefathers, the men who lived long ago. His purpose in waking them up was so that he could look for their names. He wanted to see if their names were in the Book of Life. But if their names weren’t there, if our names are not there, we’ll be very sad.

That is why Jesus told his disciples: “Go into all the world,” he said. “but don’t take a lot of clothes with you,” he said, “just your shoes,” he said. “Don’t take any money with you when you go into the world to take*Dupade*’s words to the people of the world. Just trust me for what you need.”*(Matthew 28:18-20)*

This is so that we might know if you want to go with us – and if you do, just tell our Father*Dupade*. He will forgive your sins. Trust*Dupade*as I present you to him while you are still alive. On the other hand, I might die, and other believers will tell you about this. But you could die before hearing their teaching.

I know that all of us will die, and no one anywhere will remain alive forever. When a person comes to the end of his life, that will be the end for him, and he’ll never experience another kind of life.

Therefore, the Bible says, Jesus says: “Whoever believes in me, I will give him life. A new kind of life that is everlasting. A life that will never have an end.”

I know that when you are young you’re happy, and you look forward to going hunting, or to look for honey, or to play around with girls, or to eat from your own garden. And you think your life is never going to end. But we don’t know when*Dupade*is going to arrive.

I know if you’re a young woman, you’re happy, too. You play around and mistakenly think that’s all there is to life. But there’s more to life when you decide to trust in*Dupade*. If you want*Dupade*’s state of being the only one you believe in, that becomes your life from then on.

On the other hand, others of you will think all is going well for you, but when*Dupade*’s Son returns he will punish you because Satan has been your friend. When you chose to do what Satan wanted you to do, rather than believe in*Dupade*, you chose sadness, for one day, you will reach the land that*Dupade*hates. Therefore, I have this to say to you: If you haven’t put your faith in*Dupade*yet, do it right away while there’s still time.

You have heard that Jesus is going to come back again, and when he does, there will be no more time. It will be like when someone tells his fellow-person something and that person believes what he says. That’s because he sees the one who told him this. These are*Dupade*’s words: If you want to believe in him, then do it right away. If it’s night-time or day-time do it right then before we leave. If I were talking about myself, that I had not yet taken off it wouldn’t be important, but I’m talking about*Dupade*’s Son’s arrival for he is coming. If he doesn’t come tomorrow then we’ll not yet be leaving, I won’t be leaving yet. We could leave on a Sunday or a Wednesday. You probably know when Wednesday is. But we haven’t left yet. I don’t know when it will be that we leave for*Dupade*’s city. This is all that I have to teach us. And now I will talk to*Dupade*for us.

**Ecarai****talks to****Dupade****:**

*Yoqui-**Dupade*, I am talking to you tonight because I’m very happy that you hear what we say. When we prayed to you for rain, you poured it down upon us,*Yoqui-**Dupade*, and I want to thank you for the rain you poured out. You did that, so the men would be happy with their crops and we could be well-fed and healthy,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. You did it so that they would talk to you and thank you that you love us. You showed us your love when you brought rain to us. And now they are happy that they are eating the produce from their crops. Therefore, I am talking to you right now and asking you to watch over us, those of us here in Rincón. Help us right now and give us health and strength,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. Give us love, for we talk to you in Jesus’ name. Amen.

Dupade – God

Yoqui-Dupade – Our God

*Ecarai**–**Rincon de Tigre, Bolivia**– 1960’s.*

*Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**.*

ayoreo:
Asi ome pirajatigai tome yoque jnese,*Dupade*. Asi ome pirajatigai tome
yoquijorane guidaigoso ore urasade misionéro ore,*Dupade*, banguresórone ore,*Dupade*,
ujetiga ore chiraja bajegaidode cucha,*Dupade*,
ome ore jetiga ore chacate yoquijorane guidaigose ore ome baruode to,*Dupade*. Bei baruode ome yoquijorane guidaigoso
uje yoquipotigádi u ujetiga baruode chise erámi jnese ujetiga babi Jesuse
chimate daruode iji yoque enga uté diapo rĩ ujetiga jno danguretasóningo ore
údi,*Dupade*.
english: Give understanding to all of us,*Dupade*. And give understanding to our friends, the*Guidaigosode*and to the missionaries who are with them who believe in you also. Help them to know how to teach the*Guidaigosode*about your Word also. Take your Word to our friends the*Guidaigosode*, for we want the whole world to be reached with your Word so that we all may know what your*Son Jesus*has said about coming back for his believers and taking them with him to*Dupade*’s town.

ayoreo: *Chequé Dago*ajéode jno yúdi aja darásade ga ore urejanie ore chicha yu aja darásade uje jnacare pariguéjna yu. Que yijína aja urásade. Deroco yugúchade dacaranone yui jeta yijína*Chequé Dago*.
english: But*Cheque Dago*ignored me and she slept with me.

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Echo yu. Yibai tu yiyangónique jne.”
english: *Corabé*said: “Even if the one who finds me is ugly, he’ll still be my husband.” After they found her, she said: “My husband will be the one who get’s to me first.”

ayoreo:
Jecucha yiya Dupade adode mu
yorase paraque diríca. Ore chojninga: “Urusãráquedé ga Dupade anguresõre ga
cho yoque to.” Yirajágo uje cucha omi déji gaté. Dupade ipesudi cucha uerate déji gaté. Jeta yo parágome Dupade adode, a yoco parágome Dupade, ga íjnoque Dupade ueratigai tiji yoc. Jé yocadi pitoringai jeta yoco parágome. Adi jé cho uje que yiraja cucháric. Que yirajapise cuchárigo. Jecute yingo uaque to yate bajade.
english: I would stop following*Dupade*and start making mistakes. People would say about me: “Urusãráquedé is a believer in Dupade, but he acts just like us.”

ayoreo: *Ecarai – Tobité, Bolivia – 1971**Faith Wyma churúsãre Ecari atatatgode udojo nanique*
english: And that’s what my teaching was about when I told you that*Dupade*doesn’t like lots of noise and confusion in his house. That’s because*Dupade*wants us to listen quietly to his*Words*. And then he can see that his*Words*are important to us and that we love him when we are quiet in his house.

ayoreo: Yimóji*Chícode*gari mu yajéngopis ome*Chícode*uje que catecã́ri yu. A yiyápo*Chícode*rĩ uje que catecã́ri yu ga yibágui*Ecarai*. Jé yimesẽre*Ecarai*uje jĩrique catecã́ri yu, mu chi*Chícode*chijnóra yu diríca. Que catecã́ri yu diríca mu chisapo yu rĩ ga chojninga: “Qué yajéngome ua diríca gu.”
english: I slept with*Chícode,*but I was bashful around him because he didn’t talk to me. So I broke up with him, because he didn’t talk to me, and I went to*Ecarai.*I liked*Ecarai*because he quickly started talking to me, but he told me that*Chícode*loved me.

ayoreo:
Mu Dupade
Uruode qui u uje chojninga: “Yibidi tu uaque jnese. Yipota jeta yijna ua aja
yidai to, mu uaque u uje uaquetácho yu. Que eso yunératique to,” chojninga.
english: And that is what it tells about in John 17 that Jesus prayed to his Father. He said: “Father, I’m going to leave those you gave to me here on earth, but I’ll not really be leaving them, because I’m going to leave your Word with them.” **earnestly****for those of us who would believe on him in the future:**

ayoreo: Tími, chi ayoréode chi yocãrapíode chi jec u cuchisode udore iji erámi to nanique ome jnani bajade. Ẽjé. Cuchiso ponáyone. Chi yocãrapíode u to ome jnacare bajade. Mu que ore chiraja gu. Chiese yocãrapíode cuchiso  ponáyone iji erámi to iji uté, jnacare bajade. Ẽjé. Cuchisode jnese. Chiese yocãrapíode jé u to ome jnacare bajade.
english: They told about the tapir taking an Ayoré man away with him, one of the first men told about this. The Ayoré crawled upon him one day. And the tapir provided all the different kinds of food for him. He carried the Ayoré. And he gave the Ayoré food things. The Ayoré got hungry and that is when he gave him honey to eat. And told him how to get honey. And how to chop for it. And how to suck it. The tapir said, “Why, honey is really delicious.’ And the honey gourd was filled with honey. He said he took it out of the honeycomb.

ayoreo:
Mu jé ũré Jesus, uje chi cojñone chujé ga ore chijóchame, ga que ore chimopo gotique rĩ. Mu chi Jesus ecapo rĩ enga e chiquetapo raja dacadigode uñane rĩ iji guiguíjna ajénique udojo uñeque. Ga jogadi tu uje ore irasigapo re rĩ. Ga Jesus
acajnaquéode u udojo ome yoque uje que uyoque gotique, Jesus chi acadigode u
nanique, mu jé udojo qui uté Dupade Uruode jeta yingongo ñane. Jesus chiqueta
raja origuíjna ajei uñai te ga, ore irasigápo re rĩ uje ore chimo Jesus. Ore
chingo ñane, ore chingo Dupade anguresórone uje Dupade ujnacari casica e dépoi
ore ujade rĩ. Que yimo jeta guedosírigo a gare, uje déji ore gari uje chi
Jesus ecapo nanique rĩ. Ise ga ca potigayaquérique dépoi rĩ ome anírone jnani
bajade, Jesus anguresórone. Que yiraja jetiga 40 dirode uje chi Jesus déji ore
gari, dacadi casodica ore gari, iji ore iguíjnane, ore aquiningane ga chi
chiqueta raja ore.
english: We are like that today, too; when what we teach is not understood, we must give examples to help people to understand. And that’s what Jesus did, he gave them examples. But I’m telling you, they still didn’t understand. They kept saying: “What do Jesus’ words mean? Why is he saying he’s going to leave, and that we’re not going to see him anymore? We don’t know where he’s going, we don’t understand this teaching of his?”

ayoreo:
Uje yirajágo
gu uje quigode cuse. Quigode u uje, ujetiga yipótago puru uñeque ga yangácoi.
Ga jé u uje, aníri, yirajágo uje pacajnaquéode déji gu. Enga jé areguipise iji
dirode udojo gajnerĩ uje yirajágo uje yacágo aja ñane ajéode gu, cojñone,
ayoréode ore uyóguiji erámi jnese iji dirode udojo gajnerĩ.
english: It’s true that, even when we are miles apart in understanding each other,*Dupade*knows what is in our hearts. He knows all about our sins.  And he knows what we think about His Son, and whether our thoughts are good. For*Dupade*isn’t far off, he’s right here beside us wherever we are in this world. The end.

ayoreo: *Pujnuiné – Tobité,Bolivia – 1960*
english: *Pujnuiné– Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*
