ayoreo: Qué yedobu jnacarode acoté*Chequé Dago*. Chojninga: Ẽ́ yisapié yacoté gapu.*Pujnucode*déji sañeque ga tangári yu ga chirate yu. Ca chajéngome yu jne. Chiradirase yu iji dejode gajnerĩ, yacoté casé,*Átapide*.
english: I’m going to sing about*Chequé Dago*who was the wife of young men. Supposedly, I had relations with a young girl. When*Pujnúcode*was away he heard me sing and imitated me. He didn’t respect me. He tried to imitate what I sang about a former wife called Átapide.

ayoreo:
A jé ca yujóraque chequé oyo yu
gotique, uje yetaguipise sigásodie uje ore chijna. Yitodopísa borácho yugode
rĩ. Que yipota jetiga uñeque idógosi iji yu.
english: I’m happy, because I believe in Dupade with all my heart. But this is what I am not happy about, my husband doesn’t believe in Dupade. It would be so much better if all my family were sincerely following Dupade – my husband, my children, and myself.

ayoreo: Cojñócho ayoréode. Chi ayoré Aramato, cho otadábode. Ore chujé Yamochai ga chijaro ajnoquéode ga chisite ajnoquéode. Ga pésu ayoréode adode tiji jnese, Aramato. Pésu ayoré; chijaro ajnoquéode, Yamochai ajnoquéode. Chimate ga chibote ujñari aja ajei. Chimate ga ayoré. Chi chicháji ti. Chiese cojñone yocarãpi tu Yamochai ajnoquéode.
english: Moon went down another road. Moon was first to arrive, and he caused the Ayoré people to stay dead. “Ayoré people were like the non-Ayoré people at first,” the father told his little boy. “They killed *Yamochai (Monkey) and they ground his bones and sifted them into powder. “Lizard was an Ayoré before, and had legs. And Lizard took the powder made from the ground-up Monkey’s bones and made body parts from it. She finished and then she blew breath into them. When she finished doing this, it was a person. “It’s said that she put the first man to one side, and started to make the rest. “They say that non-Ayoré people and Ayoreos all were made from the Monkey’s bones.” The father said to his little son, “Animals originated from a very large woman. Bigger than anything.”

ayoreo: Ore edópasa*pajojo.*Picanerane ore edópasa tu*sioti*te rĩ. Uje ore ayo nanique uje chósi te iji ore enochai enga Picanerane edópasa. Picanerane edópasa*cuquébi*(un tipo de pájaro). (Oidabiadé cho quique: Ayoré nanique ga jeta baro*Patricia*potarása*Ayoré Gajnui*ga uyu ga yiji – mu*Noemia*abai tu ga yãratayipis ome*Juan*uhuaque enga dire uñai enga*Patricia*cha. Mu chugúpẽre uté u iji datí. Boquere chugúpẽre. Datedie ore chojningame dabode: “Aya dara, “*cuquébi a!*” Ujetiga abi que cha go enga daté chojninga: “¡*Cuquep*!”) Yoquedópasa*coyidé*(un tipo de pájaro) uje chojninga: “*Coyi-coyi-coyi*!” Agoyé u. Que yiraja ore edópasade, cucherane jnoji yáyipie gu. Jnuriminone ore edópasa pitoringai uje yitogoi. Chi ore edópasa pitoringai. Mu que yiraja Jnuriminone edopasade to. Ujeta gosi acoté toi ga abai chojninga: “Yimope cochocoi abubie sogójma yacoté uaqué to que.” Gaidi u uje chijnorapise dacoté ga chijna edópasa ingoningájnai taja. *Oidabiade – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidabiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: Ayoré warriors when they worked themselves up to kill long ago would liken themselves to the armadillos, that’s because the armadillo was a*weapon carrier*in far off times and would dig into the ground to protect himself. They say, it was the*Chiquenone*clan possession in far off times and that’s why they would liken themselves to it.

ayoreo: Uñane chojninga: “¡Pu a! asa gajnerí. ¡Pu a! asa gajnerí.” Jiei ore iji carujnangue údi. Ore chajo údi ga guesa údi.
english: Others would encourage their relatives and try to put charms on them so they could reach the top. They would say: “You can do it*¡pu jõ!*You can do it*¡pu jõ!”*

ayoreo: Chi*Dupade*iroque bajai u Juan uñai nanique aja erámi ujetiga chatata direcaringuei ujnomei ome erámi unirisórone ore. Que direcaringuepísi u Juan uñai uté nanique, mu di gu jeta chingome direcaringuei gosi aja erámi unirisórone.*Dupade*iroque Juan uñai uté ujetiga erámi uirisórone ore changureta direcaringuei Jesute ome uté ojñane enga piquetaque ore.
english: *Dupade*, it’s said, used His*Word*who is Jesus when things were made.*Dupade*created the world by means of his agent (Messiah) that he sent into the world. The one*Dupade*sent to do this was his Son Jesus.*Dupade*made things and his sent one finished things. And life came from him also. But darkness and sin wanted to overpower*Dupade*’s agent, but it wasn’t possible to overcome*Dupade*’s sent one, because*Dupade*can’t be overcome. Jesus is*Dupade*. And sin couldn’t overcome*Dupade*’s power, for it couldn’t overcome*Dupade*. Nothing can overcome*Dupade*because he created everything, and nothing in his creation can overpower him.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Guibéjnané atatagode udojo.*
english: That was the place I would have been at the salt beds, right where they were coming. But I looked and saw*Garáijnaidái*over there. He said: “Let’s go! I’ll leave our stuff over there.”

ayoreo:
Enga adi jé u
uje Satanas chĩro Dupade Uruode iji ayoréode yocajéode. Enga chejna Dupade
Uruode iji ayoréode, cojñone yocajéode, ga yejnángo yocáyipiedie aja cuchade
iji erámi tudé, enga gajnúone u pore ojnacúcode uje sué, sué iji utácai
acadie iji jnumi.
english: **Word****to find a place in our hearts:**

ayoreo: Jecute yuruode qui u uje ise ga yoc uñeque déji jṍronguiji jeta gosi sue Dupade Uruode jnungórone ore yoquibai.
english: The one that brought*Dupade’s Words*to us long ago wasn’t an*Ayoré*. He didn’t know our language in the beginning, but when he arrived he heard our words and imitated us. But for some of us who refused*Dupade’s Words*, he came for nothing. He didn’t know what to say to those who didn’t believe, those who would say: “I don’t believe in*Dupade*. I don’t see the benefit in following*Dupade*. That’s why I don’t go to the meetings to learn about*Dupade’s Words*.”

ayoreo:
Mu gusu udore uje que ore changureta
ga ore todo yui, uje ore todo yui ujetiga pujuya ore ome pioi, jeoga pujuyaquéo
uña.  Mu Dupade ujuyaquei tu uje tṍraji
gaté jne. Que yirajágo ujetiga yodi upo rĩ, ujeta pioque. Mu yirajágopo rĩ
uje pioque a u jne, ejórique, jeta cho dajuñame jnumi jogosórone uyoque.
english: **nly****Jesus Christ****can forgive our sins****:**I wanted you to hear these words of mine,*Señora Marina*, because today we have repented with all out hearts. And we’re following Jesus for only he can forgive our sins, and it is he that rose again to give new life to his believers. He did that so that when he returns to earth for us, we will go up to meet him in the air.

ayoreo: Yisago yocugúchade. Yico garai ga yimóngo iji ta uje guedé jno. E dejac uje yimóngo iji yoquirengúi. Chaguhei chejnapise yoque. Jnanione chachẽre dati múñi que yajo cuchárique. Yico garai údi mu ore chojninga: “*Burai*ducoré chacose á.”
english: We grabbed our things. We went toward the plains and saw that the sun was going down. By the time we caught sight of our camp it was night. We were hungry. Even though the men had been scouting for food we hadn’t eaten anything. We crossed the plains, and they told us: “*Burai*and them are hiding.”

ayoreo: Yijñecarósi ji de.
english: *Pejnócode chedo dajnusiétigade ome dacotedie*

ayoreo: *Uneredaté*chojninga: “*Edoguesaide*, tágape ua. Que jnacanique etácho yu gotique.”
english: She said to her children: “Stay here. I’m going to*Guidábi*. I’m going directly there. ¿Could a young man refuse me?”

ayoreo: – ore uniri u*G**arai*nanique. (ore i ejoi tu) – ore uniri i tu*C**ucójna*bajaque, ejoi i tu*J**nupedo**Cucarãte**Cuchábode*ga jnupedo déji.
english: Their land was in the plains where there were many*bottle trees*. *Jnupedógosode –*“People of the caves.

ayoreo: **chatata uje jnani bajade que ore chiraja uje Dupade u uje déji yoquibai:**
english: He’d call out also for pools of water. And instead of rain, without knowing why,*Dupade*would place a jaguar in their path. They would kill the jaguar, for that is what*Dupade*provided, so that is what they killed. For*Dupade*was always placing things in front of them because they called upon him long ago.

ayoreo:
A yisapo Rincón dirome rĩ jeta
e Dijaide doi enga chicáji ome congreso. 
A yicopo Señora Marina dirome rĩ. Yirasique uje ore chudute yuruode. Que
toque go jetiga jé yajnésãra yiquigade. Que toque go jetiga yudute ayoréraque
urúrique ga que toguipise gu.
english: But, I’m not lying about that. It makes me so happy because he does teach our family about*Dupade*’s*Word.*And that is why I never want to turn back from where I now am. I have found myself at last, and I don’t ever want to live the way I did. Especially now, in these days since I have given myself to*Dupade*. And especially now that we have a testimony to maintain before others. There are some women who complain about*Dupade*’s*Word*, but I don’t complain about anything to do with*Dupade*.

english: You remember that some time ago I taught you about the two ways a person can be sick. Our human suffering has to do with our bodies, according to*Dupade*. On the other hand, there is a sickness that takes place in our souls and that is caused by sin. Our sin is like a sickness in our souls.*(Psalm 66:18)*
